title: French Onion Soup

ingredients.name: large onions, thinly sliced into half-rings

subs.name: 2 tbsp olive oil — gives a slightly less rich base but works well
subs.notes: the caramelisation takes the same time

ingredients.name: vegetable stock (or beef stock for non-vegetarian)

subs.name: 1 litre water with 2 vegetable stock cubes (Maggi or Knorr)

ingredients.name: thick bread slices (preferably sourdough or crusty white)

subs.name: leftover dinner rolls or thick pav — toast in a dry kadai until crisp
subs.notes: shape is less important than crispness

ingredients.name: hard cheese, grated (gruyere, emmental, or Amul cheddar)

subs.name: Amul processed cheese slices (1–2 slices per bowl) — melt perfectly under indirect heat

ingredients.name: sugar
visual_checks: helps caramelisation in the final stage
ingredients.notes: helps caramelisation in the final stage

steps.text: Melt the butter in a wide, heavy-bottomed pot or deep kadai over medium flame. Add all the sliced onions with a generous pinch of salt and stir to coat them in the butter. The onions will look like an enormous pile — they reduce dramatically.

Cook the onions over medium-low heat, stirring every 3–4 minutes, for 30–35 minutes until they are a deep amber-brown and soft throughout. Do not rush with high heat — the colour must come from true caramelisation, not burning. If the pot looks too dry, add a tablespoon of water and scrape the bottom.

Sprinkle in the sugar (if using) and cook 2 more minutes, stirring, to deepen the colour. Remove the bay leaves if you added them early. The onions are ready when they are sticky, deeply brown, and smell sweet.

Pour in the stock and add the bay leaves, thyme sprigs, and black pepper. Bring to a brisk boil then reduce to a gentle simmer. Cook uncovered for 15 minutes to let the flavours meld. Taste and adjust salt — the stock may already be salted.
Toast the bread slices until firm and golden on both sides — they need to be crisp enough to float rather than dissolve into the soup. Cut each slice to fit your serving bowl if needed.

Ladle the hot soup into oven-safe bowls, float a toast slice on each, and pile grated cheese on top. Place bowls under a grill or in a hot oven (220°C) for 3–5 minutes until the cheese is melted, golden, and beginning to bubble at the edges. Serve immediately while the cheese is still sizzling.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: cheese → Amul processed cheese slices (1–2 slices per bowl) — melt perfectly under indirect heat (or use any cheddar-style cheese); butter → 2 tbsp olive oil — gives a slightly less rich base but works well (the caramelisation takes the same time); stock → 1 litre water with 2 vegetable stock cubes (Maggi or Knorr) (good for budget cooking); bread → leftover dinner rolls or thick pav — toast in a dry kadai until crisp (shape is less important than crispness)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: A wide kadai or heavy-based pressure-cooker body works well for the long caramelisation stage. For the cheese topping without an oven, ladle the soup into heatproof steel bowls, float the toast, add grated Amul cheddar or processed cheese, then cover each bowl tightly with a lid or foil and rest for 3–4 minutes — the steam from the hot soup melts the cheese beautifully. Alternatively, briefly hold a hot tawa above the bowl to create radiant heat.
